advantages: State-of-the-art on tabular data: GBDT libraries routinely win structured ML benchmarks and production pipelines where deep nets offer little gain.
Histogram splits scale to millions of rows and high-cardinality numerics — training that is impractical with exact greedy search becomes feasible.
Built-in missing-value handling learns default split directions — predict without upfront imputation for many workflows.
Rich regularization: learning rate, tree depth/leaves, L1/L2 on leaf weights, subsampling — controls overfitting without switching model class.
Feature interactions captured automatically via sequential shallow trees — no manual cross-terms required.
Mature ecosystems: sklearn-compatible APIs, early stopping, GPU options, feature importance, and widespread industry adoption.
disadvantages: Many hyperparameters: learning_rate, n_estimators, depth/leaves, subsample rates, and regularization require validation tuning — not one-click defaults.
Leaf-wise LightGBM can overfit aggressively if num_leaves is too high without min_data_in_leaf or early stopping.
Less interpretable than a single small tree or linear model — ensembles of hundreds of trees are harder to explain to stakeholders.
Histogram binning quantizes splits — extreme precision on continuous thresholds can be lost (usually minor).
Poor fit for raw images, audio, or unstructured text without careful feature engineering — neural models dominate those modalities.
Training is sequential across boosting rounds (though tree construction parallelizes) — less embarrassingly parallel than Random Forests at the forest level.
